title: The Space Race Comes to Latin America
gist: Latin America is emerging as a contested frontier in the global space economy, with Ecuador, the Dominican Republic, Brazil and Peru courting spaceport investment amid a US-China rivalry for ground infrastructure, even as weak institutions, thin budgets, and Alcântara's troubled history temper the hype.

insights.claim: Countries near the equator, including Ecuador, Colombia and Brazil, offer cheaper satellite launches because Earth's rotational speed is highest there (about 1,040 mph/1,670 km/h at the equator), letting rockets carry more payload for less fuel.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Physical geography is a durable, non-replicable cost advantage — equatorial launch sites deserve a structural premium over higher-latitude competitors.
insights.quote: The planet rotates at its maximum speed at the equator—1,040 miles (1,670 kilometers) per hour—making it more efficient to launch rockets from these latitudes.
insights.locator: The planet rotates at its maximum speed

insights.claim: Entrepreneur Robert Aillón's Leviathan Space Industries is scouting a private spaceport near Guayaquil, Ecuador, roughly two degrees south of the equator, and is trying to raise $800 million starting this year, with Florida-based Blackstar Orbital secured as its first partner for cargo/surveillance space drones.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: An early-stage, unfunded $800M raise signals the sector is still speculative — track whether capital actually closes before treating Ecuador spaceport plans as real.
insights.locator: For Leviathan's initiative, Aillón said

insights.claim: Ecuador's investment case is complicated by organized-crime groups battling for control of its seaports, which are key transit hubs for cocaine bound for Europe and Asia.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Security risk is a real discount factor on Ecuador's equatorial advantage — diligence on port/logistics security is necessary before backing any Ecuadorian spaceport play.
insights.locator: Some foreign investors regard Ecuador with caution

insights.claim: The global space economy could reach $1.8 trillion by 2035, up from $630 billion in 2023, per a World Economic Forum/McKinsey & Company report.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A roughly 3x expansion over a decade frames the space economy as a durable multi-year secular growth theme, not a one-off.
insights.locator: A report by the World Economic Forum and McKinsey

insights.claim: SpaceX's June IPO raised a record $75 billion, further igniting global interest in the space sector, and Musk has said he will look beyond the U.S. for future spaceports, potentially benefiting Latin America.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: SpaceX's stated intent to site future launch infrastructure outside the U.S. is a direct catalyst that could re-rate Latin American spaceport candidates.
insights.locator: The June initial public offering Elon Musk's SpaceX

insights.claim: The European Space Agency projects the number of orbiting satellites will grow from about 10,000 today to 100,000 by 2030, much of it small satellites in constellations for internet services, a market Amazon is also poised to enter.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A 10x jump in satellite count implies a corresponding surge in demand for launch capacity and ground infrastructure — a tailwind for new launch-site entrants.
insights.locator: The ESA projects the number of satellites

insights.claim: Brazil's Alcântara Launch Center (CLA), created in 1983 just 2.3 degrees south of the equator, has been underutilized for years after a 2003 rocket explosion killed 21 engineers and technicians on the launch pad.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A proven equatorial site with a two-decade underutilization history is a cautionary comp for how long it can take Latin American spaceports to reach commercial scale.
insights.locator: Following a rocket explosion on a CLA launch pad

insights.claim: Alcântara saw its first commercial launch in December 2025 from South Korean firm Innospace, which crashed under a minute after liftoff; Innospace still plans to launch from the CLA again this year.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: An early failed commercial launch is a near-term reliability red flag for Alcântara as a commercial launch destination.
insights.locator: Alcântara saw its first commercial launch

insights.claim: Brazil's 2024 space activities law and the 2025-launched state company ALADA (Empresa de Projetos Aeroespaciais do Brasil) aim to give legal security to launch operators and strengthen the Brazilian space program, but the country allocates only $47 million to space (0.002% of 2023 GDP), the second-lowest funding level among G20 countries.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Legal reform without funding is a familiar pattern — expect slow, capital-constrained progress in Brazil's space buildout despite the new law.
insights.locator: Despite this renewed momentum, lack of funding remains a challenge

insights.claim: The Dominican Republic is building a spaceport in Pedernales via a $600 million agreement with U.S. firm Launch on Demand (LOD Holding), with a first launch targeted for May 2028, financed by private institutional investors rather than public funds.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A privately financed, dated project with a named operator and named site is one of the more concrete, trackable Latin American spaceport bets to monitor for milestones.
insights.quote: We are going to launch a satellite or a rocket into space from Pedernales—just wait and see.
insights.locator: President Luis Abinader

insights.claim: LOD Holding CEO Burton Catledge, a retired U.S. Air Force colonel who supported launches for SpaceX, Blue Origin and Moon Express, chose Pedernales for its proximity to the equator (18°N), open-water access, low population density, favorable winds, and multiple orbital trajectories, plus government-backed seaport and airport expansion nearby.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Site-selection logic backed by an experienced former USAF space-launch operator adds credibility relative to less-vetted regional proposals.
insights.locator: In an interview with AQ, Catledge explained

insights.claim: The Dominican Republic project envisions four launch pads (two for heavy/superheavy rockets, one medium, one small) operating independently, and has direct political backing from the U.S. government as part of its push to expand Western Hemisphere launch sites against China.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Explicit U.S. government backing suggests policy tailwinds (permitting, diplomatic support) beyond the private financing already secured.
insights.quote: a tangible commitment from the U.S. that they're not just saying they're pivoting to the Western Hemisphere—they in fact are.
insights.locator: The Launch on Demand CEO said the project also has political backing

insights.claim: Peru is pursuing an 'old space,' state-led spaceport model: its Ministry of Defense-run space agency signed a November 2024 MOU with NASA for feasibility studies, and a 2023-2026 Multi-Year Investment Report estimates project cost at $270 million, with Talara (four degrees south of the equator) the leading candidate site over Chiclayo or Arequipa.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A NASA-backed, government-costed project with a specific dollar figure and shortlisted sites gives investors a concrete Peru entry point to track versus Ecuador's still-informal private pitch.
insights.locator: The Ministry of Defense's 2023–2026 Multi-Year Investment Report

insights.claim: In March, Peru's Congress passed by a wide margin a bill (Law 32571) declaring the spaceport project a matter of national interest, authorizing the Ministry of Defense to lead technical and legal viability studies.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Legislative consensus despite Peru's chronic political instability signals unusually durable cross-party support for the project.
insights.locator: Even the Peruvian Congress overcame its chronic divisions

insights.claim: A U.S. congressional committee report found China has built 11 facilities for satellite communications and radio telescopes in Latin America (including Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil and Venezuela), raising U.S. concern over dual-use military potential given Beijing's fused civil-military space doctrine.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: Chinese ground-infrastructure buildout in the region is already substantial (11 sites) and is the concrete trigger for U.S. pushback — expect continued U.S.-China friction over any new site announcements.
insights.locator: A recent U.S. congressional committee report noted

insights.claim: The Trump administration is applying an updated Monroe Doctrine to pressure Latin American countries against hosting new Chinese space installations; Argentina's CESCO radio telescope in San Juan Province sits inactive and Chile canceled an observatory project atop Cerro Ventarrones in the Atacama, both attributed to U.S. pressure.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: U.S. pressure has already killed two concrete Chinese projects — a template likely to repeat, meaning new Chinese space deals in the region face real geopolitical execution risk.
insights.locator: To block new initiatives, the Trump administration

insights.claim: Chinese satellite operators (Guo Wang under China Satellite Network Group, and Qianfan under Shanghai Spacesail Technologies) filed requests in the final week of 2025 with the ITU to deploy over 200,000 satellites, a volume that has already pushed China to explore an equatorial spaceport in Malaysia, with some analysts speculating South America could be next.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: If China does pursue a South American equatorial launch site, it would sharply escalate the U.S.-China contest for regional space infrastructure — worth monitoring for early signals.
insights.locator: several Chinese satellite operators filed requests

insights.claim: Victoria Valdivia of the European Space Policy Institute warns Latin American 'new space' startups tend to prioritize visibility and business pitches over the regulatory groundwork (environmental permits, flight and tax rules) needed to actually operate, given the region's slow bureaucracies.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A credentialed skeptic flags regulatory execution risk as the real bottleneck, not technology or capital — a useful discount factor when evaluating flashy Latin American space startup pitches.
insights.quote: Because their projects are attractive, they receive a great deal of visibility, but since they are more focused on business than on regulations, they forget that they need an entire organizational framework to operate
insights.locator: Valdivia said

insights.claim: Laura Delgado López of Florida International University cautions that Latin American space opportunities 'will not materialize automatically' and require better understanding of the sector's underlying mechanisms.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A regional space-policy expert explicitly pushes back on gold-rush framing — treat bullish regional space narratives with skepticism absent institutional follow-through.
insights.quote: Opportunities exist, yes, but they will not materialize automatically.
insights.locator: said Laura Delgado López

insights.claim: Leviathan Space CEO Robert Aillón cites Scotland's SaxaVord Spaceport, built with just $57 million in initial investment and set for a suborbital launch this summer, as a low-cost comparison point for barriers to entry.
insights.speaker: Juan Pablo Toro
insights.so_what: A $57M comparable versus Leviathan's $800M target suggests either significant scope difference or capital inefficiency worth scrutinizing in the Ecuador pitch.
insights.locator: Aillón, the Leviathan Space CEO, cited the example
